chunks.title: ISEN 350 Syllabus
chunks.content:
Section 901 (26578)
Quality Engineering
Spring 2026 - College Station
Credit Hours: 3

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Quality Engineering. (2-3). Credit 3. Strategic approach to implementing quality, process and business improvement methods using data analysis tools; total quality management and six sigma approaches to define, measure, analyze, improve and control processes; principles of lean engineering; control charts; process capability analysis; basic metrology, applied statistics, lean principles and process capability. Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in ISEN 310 and ISEN 230.

chunks.title: Additional Course Details
chunks.content:
ISEN 350 will follow a flipped classroom format. Classes will meet on Wednesdays, and next week's module will be posted at 1 PM. Weekly modules begin with an overview and include lecture videos, a reading assignment, a problem set, and a module quiz. Most lecture videos are brief—approximately 20 minutes—and are designed to introduce key concepts. While they are not comprehensive, they will serve as a foundation for each module and will guide your study. Further amplification of the material will be found in the textbook, reference materials, and classroom discussions.

Success in this course requires active engagement. You are fully responsible for your own learning and performance. I am here to support and guide you, but you must take initiative.

Wednesday class meetings are for you and will be used to answer questions, review critical concepts, work through examples, develop problem-solving skills, and discuss challenging problems from the current problem set. These sessions are essential to your understanding of the material and performing well in this class.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in ISEN 310 and ISEN 230.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

* Apply Six Sigma methodologies to improve process quality
* Identify waste and reduce non-value-added activities in processes
* Apply statistical concepts and data analytic methods to reduce product and service system variability
* Think critically, develop computer codes, and write technical reports
* Implement the methods and tools of Lean-Six Sigma in engineering practice
* Write professional and informative technical reports

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content:
UWRT - University Required Writing Intensive

ISEN 350 is now certified as a Writing (W) course. In ISEN 350, you'll put Quality Engineering methods into practice through hands-on lab activities and document your findings in professional technical reports. Because this is a Writing (W) course, you'll also develop strong written communication skills that are highly valued by employers. Faculty from the ISEN Writing and Student Success Center will be available to guide and support you in becoming a more confident and effective technical communicator.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Introduction to Statistical Quality Control
Authors: Douglas C. Montgomrty
Publisher: Wiley
Edition: Eighth

chunks.title: Notes:
chunks.content:
Statistics Capable (hand-held) Calculator: (Ex. TI 84 Plus, TI 89, Nspire, etc.) is required. Be sure to bring your calculator to all Lectures & Labs.

Software: MATLAB, Python (equivalent programming language), EXCEL, and Minitab. (Minitab instructions provided in Lab)

chunks.title: Course Materials & Copyright:
chunks.content: All documents used in this course are copyrighted. The word "documents" means all materials generated for this class, including, but not limited to, syllabi, quizzes, exams, problem sets, slide sets, videos, and all materials appearing on the class website or sent to you via email. Because these materials are copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy or share any of them for any purpose other than your personal academic use unless I expressly grant permission

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Course Grading

| | |
|:---|:---|
| Test1 | 27% |
| Final Examination | 27% |
| Lab/Projects (Writing) | 25% |
| Module Quizzes | 10% |
| Problem Sets (Writing) | 10% |
| Autobiography (Writing) | 1% |

Course Grade Assignment

90% - 100% A
80% - 89.9% B
70% - 79.9% C
60% - 69.9% D
Below - 59.9% F

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
The following late penalties apply:

* 1 day late: 25% deduction
* 2 days late: 50% deduction
* 3 or more days late: Grade of zero
* Plan ahead to avoid penalties.

chunks.title: College and Department Policies
chunks.content:
Following Department Policy, I will request the Aggie Honor Court to assign a grade of F* for any breach of the Aggie Honor Code, no matter how minimal the offense may be.
Because writing, analysis, and critical thinking are core learning outcomes of this course:

* All written work, computer code, and problem-solving assignments must be your own work and completed individually
* Collaboration of any kind is not permitted

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content: Lab attendance is mandatory to receive credit for the lab assignment. Missing two lab assignments will result in a final course grade of F.
